title_en: At-Tabarani and the Three Mu'jam Collections
content_id:
Sulayman ibn Ahmad ibn Ayyub al-Lakhmi at-Tabarani lahir pada tahun 260 H di Tabariyyah (Tiberias), kota yang menjadi asal nisbah beliau, di tepi barat Danau Galilea di Palestina bersejarah. Beliau menjadi salah satu penyusun hadis terpenting pada abad keempat kalender Islam, dikenal terutama karena tiga koleksi mu'jam ensiklopedis beliau: Al-Mu'jam al-Kabir (Leksikon Besar), Al-Mu'jam al-Awsat (Leksikon Tengah), dan Al-Mu'jam al-Saghir (Leksikon Kecil). Ketiga karya ini secara kolektif merupakan salah satu kompilasi hadis terorganisir terbesar dalam sejarah ilmu keislaman.

At-Tabarani menuntut ilmu dengan dedikasi luar biasa sepanjang kehidupan ilmiah yang sangat panjang. Beliau memulai perjalanan studinya pada usia tiga belas tahun dan melanjutkannya selama beberapa dekade, akhirnya berguru kepada lebih dari seribu ulama di seluruh dunia Islam — Suriah, Hijaz, Mesir, Irak, Persia, Yaman, dan lainnya. Jaringan guru yang luas ini memberi beliau akses ke sanad-sanad yang tidak dikumpulkan secara sistematis oleh kolektor lain, dan koleksi mu'jam beliau menjadi gudang material hadis yang tak ternilai yang tidak tersedia di tempat lain.

Istilah mu'jam merujuk pada genre koleksi hadis tertentu di mana tradisi disusun secara abjad berdasarkan nama Sahabat tertinggi dalam sanad — pada intinya, tradisi-tradisi dikelompokkan berdasarkan Sahabat yang menjadi sumber akhirnya. Ketiga mu'jam at-Tabarani berbeda dalam cakupan: Al-Mu'jam al-Kabir memuat tradisi para Sahabat secara abjad dan mencakup sekitar dua puluh lima jilid dalam cetakan modern; Al-Mu'jam al-Awsat mengorganisir tradisi berdasarkan guru-guru at-Tabarani sendiri; dan Al-Mu'jam al-Saghir adalah pilihan yang lebih singkat.

At-Tabarani menetap di Isfahan di masa tuanya dan wafat di sana pada tahun 360 H pada usia yang luar biasa, yaitu seratus tahun. Kehidupannya yang panjang dan aktivitas pengumpulannya yang luar biasa menjadikan beliau penyusun hadis terkemuka pada eranya di dunia Islam timur, dan tiga koleksi mu'jam beliau telah dikutip sebagai sumber oleh hampir semua ulama hadis terkemuka pada abad-abad berikutnya.
content_en:
Sulayman ibn Ahmad ibn Ayyub al-Lakhmi at-Tabarani was born in 260 AH in Tabariyyah (Tiberias), the city from which his nisba is derived, on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee in historical Palestine. He became one of the most important hadith compilers of the fourth century of the Islamic calendar, known above all for his three encyclopedic mu'jam collections: al-Mu'jam al-Kabir (The Large Lexicon), al-Mu'jam al-Awsat (The Middle Lexicon), and al-Mu'jam as-Saghir (The Small Lexicon). These three works collectively constitute one of the largest organized hadith compilations in the history of Islamic scholarship.

At-Tabarani pursued knowledge with extraordinary dedication across an exceptionally long scholarly life. He began his study travels at the age of thirteen and continued them for decades, eventually studying under more than a thousand teachers across the breadth of the Islamic world — Syria, the Hijaz, Egypt, Iraq, Persia, Yemen, and beyond. This vast network of teachers gave him access to chains of transmission that other collectors had not systematically gathered, and his mu'jam collections became invaluable repositories of hadith material unavailable elsewhere.

The term mu'jam refers to a specific genre of hadith collection in which traditions are organized alphabetically according to the name of the highest Companion in the chain — essentially, traditions are grouped by the Companion from whom they ultimately derive. The three mu'jam of at-Tabarani differ in scope: al-Mu'jam al-Kabir contains the traditions of the Companions in alphabetical order and runs to approximately twenty-five volumes in modern print; al-Mu'jam al-Awsat organizes traditions by at-Tabarani's own teachers; and al-Mu'jam as-Saghir is a shorter selection.

At-Tabarani settled in Isfahan in his later years and died there in 360 AH at the extraordinary age of one hundred years. His long life and his prodigious collection activity made him the preeminent hadith compiler of his era in the eastern Islamic world, and his three mu'jam collections have been cited as sources by virtually every major hadith scholar of the subsequent centuries.
